patient_utterances: [Clinician asked: "Hi."] Hi.
[Clinician asked: "Hi. How are you?"] Uh, , yeah. I'm all right, thanks. Um, though, I'm actually feeling a bit wheezy.
[Clinician asked: "OK. My name is Doctor ."] Like <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/>.
[Clinician asked: "I'm one of <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> that work for Babylon. Can I confirm your name and date of birth, please?"] Uh, yeah. My, my name is, um, uh, Tina Smith. And, I'm forty years old.
[Clinician asked: "OK. And, are you in a space that's quiet and private?"] Uh, yeah, yeah, yeah. I'm just, I'm <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/>.
[Clinician asked: "OK. And, you mentioned that you are wheezy."] Mm-hmm.

[Clinician asked: "Can you tell me a little bit more about that?"] Yeah. So, um, it started, like, about two days ago. Um, I don't know if it's because, you know, I was, like, cleaning the house. And, like, um, all this dust came out of the, the carpet. And, you know, I've always  thought that maybe I've got, uh, an allergy to, like, dust. Um, I think so. So, if it was triggered by Um, you know, two days later. Still not gonna be better. So. Um, just getting a little bit worried about it now.
[Clinician asked: "OK. So, you were cleaning the house  two days ago. And, that's when it first started?"] Uh, yeah. That's right.
[Clinician asked: "Did the wheezing start suddenly, or was it gradual?"] Uh, so, it started after maybe, uh, an hour or so  .
[Clinician asked: "And, has it got worse over the past two days?"] Yeah. So,  it get's a bit worse, um, at night, I find.
[Clinician asked: "OK. And, have you taken anything for the wheeze?"] Uh, no. No, I haven't actually.

[Clinician asked: "OK. And so, and the wheeze has been getting worse. And, today you're particularly worried. Is it because you're finding it hard to breathe or finding it hard to talk?"] Yeah, yeah. A bit of both actually. So, um, I'm feeling like a little bit, uh, uh, short of breath.
[Clinician asked: "OK."] Um, and, actually, when I'm, you know, talking, um, to my friends, I start finding that, uh, sometimes, um, I'm unable to , complete my sentences.
[Clinician asked: "OK."] Um, it can, it can get really bad, yeah.
[Clinician asked: "And, when you can breathe, and you take a deep breath, do you notice any pain?"] Um. Mmm, no, no. No pain.
[Clinician asked: "OK. And, have you noticed yourself feeling dizzy or the rooms spinning more than normal?"] Uh, no. No dizziness. Uh, none of that.
[Clinician asked: "OK. And, have you been eating and drinking OK, particularly water?"] Yeah, yeah. I think I'm having, like, you know,  four or five glasses a day. Yeah.

[Clinician asked: "OK. And, are you normally fit and well? Or do you have any other medical problems?"] Um. Well, actually, uh, I used to have, like, a bit of, um, asthma, uh, as a child. Um, and, also in the summer I sometimes get, um, hay fever.
[Clinician asked: "And, when you get hay fever, do you take any medications for it?"] Uh. Not really.

[Clinician asked: "OK. And, your asthma, did it resolve when you were a child?"] Uh, I think mostly, yeah. I mean, sometimes when I, um, run for, like, long periods, it can, it can start up a bit. But, um, I have, uh, I have, like, an asthma inhaler. I use that.
[Clinician asked: "OK. So, do you still have your inhalers?"] Um, I think I might have one from, like, a couple of months ago.
[Clinician asked: "OK."] Um.
[Clinician asked: "But, you don't see your doctor regularly for asthma?"] Uh, no, I don't.
[Clinician asked: "OK. And, apart from this, do you know if you're allergic to anything?"] Uh, not that I know of.
[Clinician asked: "OK. And, how far are you away from a doctor's surgery at the moment?"] Sorry, could you repeat that?
[Clinician asked: "How far are you away from a doctor's surgery at the moment?"] Uh,  they may be minutes' drive away.

[Clinician asked: "OK, good. So, given that you've had a history of asthma when you were a child, And, you get hay fever sometimes in the summer. Your shortness of breath. Hi. I've lost, um, video signal. Can you still hear me?"] Um, yeah. We just turned off.

[Clinician asked: "<INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> So, given you've got a history of asthma and hay fever. This could be one of two things. But, we want to rule out asthma or an allergy first. So, what you'll need <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> is visit one of our clinics, so we can do some more tests."] OK.
[Clinician asked: "Such as check your ."] OK. Well what sort?
[Clinician asked: "So, the first thing we would do, is use a small device on your finger, to check the amount of oxygen in your blood."] OK.
[Clinician asked: "And, from that we can tell if it's asthma or something else."] Right, OK.

[Clinician asked: "Before this happens, if you've got something, like an  antihistamine, which you normally take for hay fever at home, I would take one, just in case it is an allergy you've had from the dust."] OK. Uh, yeah. I don't have any antihistamines at home, unfortunately.
[Clinician asked: "You might be able to get one on your way to the doctor's surgery. If not, they can prescribe you one."] OK, OK. So, I can just get it over the counter then?
[Clinician asked: "Yes. And it, they're normally about four to six pounds."] Great.

[Clinician asked: "Is there anything else that you're particularly worried about, you want to ask me?"] Um, um, not really. I mean. I have, before, taken steroids for asthma. Um, I don't know if that's relevant.
[Clinician asked: "Yes, that is. And, you may need to take some steroids now. But, we cannot, we, we won't be able to know until we've done those tests first."] OK, OK.

[Clinician asked: "Cool. So, I'm gonna write a letter for your GP, just to let him know what's happened. And, the consultation with the GP in person shouldn't take too long."] All right. How long do you think it will take?
[Clinician asked: "Probably ten minutes."] OK, that's great.
[Clinician asked: "Cool. Um, you should get the letter over the app. And then, we can, you can take yourself to the GP."] All right. Thank you very much.
[Clinician asked: "Cool. OK, bye."] OK. Thank you. Bye bye.
gold_note:
PC : sobar pmhx of childhood asthma and hayfever.

HPC. 2 day history of wheezing and SOBAR, after cleaning dusty environment
No chest pain. No nausea,no vomiting, no problems e+d
SOB worse at night
Now having problems speaking in full sentences


PMH: As above.

Dhx : allergy nils no regular medications. 

DDx : acute exacerbation of asthma, ?? allergy

Plan : antihistamine and further examination and investigation.

naive.response_text:
I understand how concerning your symptoms must be, especially with the wheezing getting worse and making it hard to breathe and talk. It’s good you’re taking it seriously. Based on your history of asthma and hay fever, this could be an allergic reaction, possibly from the dust during cleaning. The clinician recommended visiting a clinic for tests, which is a good step to confirm what’s going on. If you can, take an antihistamine on your way, as it might help if it’s an allergy. The GP visit shouldn’t take long, and the letter we’re sending will keep them informed.

Please know you’re not alone in this, and the steps you’re taking are the right ones. If you have any more questions or concerns before your appointment, don’t hesitate to reach out. We’re here to support you through this. Take care, and I’ll be thinking of you as you get this sorted.

strong_prompt.response_text:
Thank you for sharing your concerns with me, Tina. It makes sense that you're feeling worried about your breathing getting worse, especially since it's affecting your ability to talk and complete sentences. I can understand how unsettling it must be to experience this after cleaning and possibly being exposed to dust, which you suspect might be triggering your symptoms.

Given your history of childhood asthma and seasonal hay fever, it's important to consider whether this could be a flare-up of asthma or an allergic reaction. The wheezing and shortness of breath you're experiencing are red flags that need to be evaluated. I recommend using your asthma inhaler if you have it nearby, as it can help alleviate symptoms. If you don't have an inhaler, or if you don't have antihistamines at home, you might want to pick some up on your way to see a doctor, as they can help if this is an allergic reaction.

We should plan to have you seen by a GP soon for further evaluation. They may check your oxygen levels and possibly take a chest X-ray to rule out other causes. Depending on the findings, they might recommend steroids or other treatments. I'll send a summary of our conversation to your GP so they can be prepared for your visit.

Before you go, could you summarize what we've agreed on? That way, I can make sure everything is clear. We'll follow up in one week to see how you're doing and adjust the plan as needed. Please don't hesitate to reach out if you have any more questions or concerns in the meantime.
